name: XCF Global, Inc.

profile.description: XCF Global, Inc. manufactures and sells renewable fuels in the United States, developing and operating clean fuel production facilities to produce sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), renewable diesel, and renewable naphtha. Its flagship asset is New Rise Renewables Reno, a facility with 38-40 million gallon per year permitted nameplate capacity in Nevada. The company targets 2027 net revenue of $110-120M and EBITDA of $65-70M from 40-43 million gallons of production. CEO Chris Cooper joined November 2025 from Neste U.S., and the company is pursuing a three-party merger with DevvStream and Southern Energy Renewables.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (0/3): XCF Global (formerly Crixus BH3 Acquisition Corp, a SPAC) only began commercial SAF production at New Rise Reno in February 2025 [1]. While commissioning the facility is a milestone, the plant required a post-commissioning shutdown and restart is now targeted for June 2026, significantly undermining execution credibility. There is no multi-year track record of successful product launches or transformative business model changes qualifying under the 5-year evidence standard. AI Deployment (0/2): No AI-powered products, AI-driven operational savings, or quantifiable AI deployment strategy have been disclosed in any public filing or press release [1][2]. A non-binding MOU with DevvStream and IP3 to explore integrating small modular reactor power with e-SAF production is purely exploratory with no commercial AI revenue. Market Share (1/2): XCF is one of very few operating domestic U.S. SAF producers at a time when demand structurally far exceeds supply; binding offtake agreements with BGN INTL and a supply agreement with Phillips 66 demonstrate early commercial traction [1]. Total production of 2.5 million gallons to date is negligible relative to U.S. jet fuel consumption, and no category leadership data exists. Product Pipeline (1/2): The company has articulated specific 2027 targets of $110-120M net revenue and 40-43 million gallons of renewable fuel production tied to a named June 2026 restart timeline and named offtake partners [1]. These are more than vague R&D promises, but execution of the restart is the critical gating factor. R&D Intensity (0/1): No R&D expenditure figures are publicly disclosed; the company's investment is focused on facility operations, and no evidence of above-industry-average R&D intensity was found [2].

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): XCF Global's purpose of decarbonizing aviation through domestic, waste-based SAF production is clear and socially relevant, linking energy security and climate action in a way that goes beyond generic corporate ESG language [4]. CEO Chris Cooper articulated the mission at ABLC2026 as building a domestic SAF system that is 'reliable, repeatable, and ready now,' framing the company's work as both a decarbonization solution and a national energy security priority [2]. The mission is genuine but has not been validated through sustained multi-year execution or navigated through leadership crisis — a score of 2 reflects a clear but not yet distinctive track record. Community Impact (0/2): No charitable programs, community investment, or foundation activity were identified; the 53-employee company is entirely focused on production restart and capital management. Employee Retention (0/2): Rapid C-suite turnover — CEO replaced November 2025, CFO removed April 9, 2026, Chief Accounting Officer resigned April 30, 2026 — signals deep organizational instability inconsistent with a mission-driven, high-retention culture [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): The conversion of $16.7M in accounts payable to equity in May 2026 reflects financial stakeholder commitment to keeping the company operational [3]. This is a debt restructuring transaction rather than open-market purchasing reflecting positive conviction, but it does signal some willingness to stay invested.

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Infrastructure is scored mechanically from available yfinance metrics. The financial picture is severely stressed across every available data point. Debt-to-Equity of 678.1% dramatically exceeds the floor of 300%, scoring 0.0 — the company converted $16.7M of accounts payable to equity in April 2026 to reduce the debt burden, entered a forbearance agreement with its ground lease landlord through January 2027, and carries a TTM net loss of approximately $127 million against $20.8M in TTM revenue [1][2][3]. ROE of 1.87% reflects near-zero capital productivity against a 15% target, scoring 0.125 at 2× weight for the growth sleeve. Profit margin of 3.56% is extremely thin, scoring 0.178. Forward P/E is negative at -2.09, indicating the market projects continued losses — negative forward P/E is treated as null and excluded from the calculation. Revenue growth YoY, 5-year CAGR, and Forward PEG are all unavailable due to the company's minimal operating history. Weighted sum of available metrics: D/E (1×: 0.0) + ROE (2×: 0.125) + Profit Margin (1×: 0.178) = 0.427; max weight = 4; infrastructure score = round(0.427/4 × 10) = 1. Liquidity floor passes: $0.40 × 20.96M avg daily volume = $8.38M daily liquidity, above the $1M threshold.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): XCF Global's core business — converting waste-based feedstocks (used cooking oil, animal fats) into SAF, renewable diesel, and renewable naphtha through the hydrotreated esters and fatty acids (HEFA) thermochemical pathway — is a physical, capital-intensive manufacturing operation with no meaningful AI substitution risk [4]. Airlines require physical fuel molecules to fly; ICAO CORSIA mandates, EU blending requirements, and corporate net-zero commitments structurally support SAF demand regardless of AI advancement. No analyst reports flag AI as a disruption risk for SAF producers; the primary risks XCF faces are financial (debt, lender disputes) and operational (facility reliability), not technological displacement [2][5]. Elevated jet fuel prices at $4.18/gallon in April 2026 favor SAF economics [4]. Over a very long horizon, AI-driven aviation efficiency improvements could reduce fuel consumption growth, but this is a slow-moving indirect pressure, not near-term disruption. AI Opportunity (0/1): XCF Global does not generate any revenue from AI-related products or services. The DevvStream MOU exploring SMR/e-SAF/AI data center co-location is early-stage exploration with no commercial revenue [1].

infinite_game.summary: XCF Global has articulated a genuine purpose around SAF and domestic energy security, and CEO Chris Cooper brings real industry credibility from Neste U.S. However, the company's operational reality — facility shutdown requiring restart, a TTM net loss of $127 million, debt threatening foreclosure, and three C-suite departures in 12 months — reflects a survival-mode organization rather than one confidently pursuing a long-term Just Cause. Just Cause earns 2 points for a genuine, strategy-shaping purpose not yet validated through sustained execution; Courage earns 1 point for taking the helm in distress; Existential Flexibility earns 1 point for evolving from SPAC to SAF operator; Trusting Teams and Worthy Rivals score 0 given organizational instability and no competitor acknowledgment. Total: 4 points, mindset FINITE.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): XCF's mission — making sustainable aviation fuel reliable, repeatable, and available now from domestic waste-based feedstocks — is genuine, differentiating, and clearly shapes strategic decisions [2][4]. CEO Cooper articulated this as a national energy security imperative at ABLC2026, positioning domestic SAF as a strategic alternative to imported petroleum and a decarbonization solution with immediate deployability. The cause is inclusive, service-oriented, and idealistic. However, with only 15 months of operating history and an operational failure already recorded, this cause has not been validated through sustained multi-cycle execution — the standard for 3 points requires decades of consistent strategic decisions tracing back to the cause.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): CEO Cooper joined XCF Global in November 2025 during a period of severe financial and operational stress — accepting leadership of a company with active lender disputes, near-delisting, and a shutdown production facility reflects willingness to lead under adversity [2][5]. The decision to pursue operational restart in June 2026 rather than concede the asset, and converting $16.7M in payables to equity to buy time, represents a bet on the mission over immediate survival optics [3]. However, these decisions reflect necessity as much as principle. A pattern of Sinek-style courageous leadership — multiple examples of sacrificing profitable revenue for long-term capability — has not yet had the opportunity to form.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Three senior leadership changes in 12 months — CEO replaced November 2025, CFO removed April 9, 2026, Chief Accounting Officer resigned April 30, 2026 — signal deep organizational instability [2]. With only 53 employees navigating going-concern conditions, debt restructuring, facility downtime, and leadership turnover simultaneously, this company cannot credibly claim the Circle of Safety Sinek identifies as foundational. No employee tenure, retention, or cultural practice data was publicly available to offset these red flags.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): No public evidence was found of XCF Global explicitly referencing SAF competitors — Neste, World Energy, TotalEnergies, or LanzaJet — as worthy rivals whose strengths reveal XCF's own weaknesses and drive improvement [1][2]. The hiring of CEO Cooper from Neste implicitly acknowledges that established players have operational capabilities XCF must develop, but this is talent acquisition, not the competitive mindset Sinek describes where rivals are named and used to sharpen your own strategy.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): XCF Global evolved from Crixus BH3 Acquisition Corp (a blank-check SPAC with no operating business) into an operational sustainable aviation fuel producer — a meaningful transformation involving a physical production facility, regulatory compliance, and commercial offtake agreements [1]. The pursuit of a three-party business combination with Southern Energy Renewables and DevvStream shows willingness to evolve the model beyond a single-facility SAF producer. These pivots demonstrate incremental flexibility and adaptation under pressure. However, since the SPAC had no core business to abandon, this does not fully qualify as Sinek's 'blow up what's working today in service of what the cause demands tomorrow' — the change was strategic formation, not existential reinvention.

investment_thesis: XCF Global operates in a structurally attractive market — SAF demand is supported by ICAO CORSIA mandates, EU blending requirements, and U.S. energy security priorities — and targets $110-120M in 2027 net revenue and $65-70M EBITDA from 40-43M gallons of domestic SAF production at New Rise Renewables Reno [1]. The June 2026 facility restart, binding BGN INTL offtake agreement, Phillips 66 supply agreement, and the three-party merger with Southern Energy Renewables and DevvStream could collectively validate the business model and unlock a diversified renewable fuel platform [1][3]. Jet fuel prices at $4.18/gallon in April 2026 improve SAF economics, and CEO Cooper's tenure as President of Neste U.S. — the world's largest SAF producer — provides genuine operational credibility [2][4].
thesis_continued: The risks are severe and immediate. D/E of 678.1% reflects a company in financial distress — $16.7M in payables were converted to equity in April 2026 to avoid covenant breach, the company entered a forbearance agreement on its ground lease through January 2027, and received a Nasdaq minimum bid price non-compliance notice requiring the stock to trade above $1.00 by June 8, 2026 [2][3]. Three C-suite departures in 12 months — CEO, CFO, and Chief Accounting Officer — raise serious execution credibility concerns for a 53-person company. Revenue and 5-year CAGR data are unavailable because the company has minimal operating history at scale. The TTM net loss of $127 million against $20.8M in TTM revenue reveals a cash-burning pre-scale operation. At $0.40/share and $119M market cap, the equity is a speculative option on successful facility restart — every financial metric in the IOWN framework fails the durability standard. SELL reflects present financial reality, not long-term market opportunity.
key_catalysts: Successful June 2026 restart of New Rise Renewables Reno and initial commercial deliveries under the BGN INTL offtake agreement — the single most critical near-term proof point; without this, 2027 revenue targets are unachievable

Closing the three-party business combination with Southern Energy Renewables and DevvStream, diversifying the platform and potentially unlocking new capital sources and environmental credit revenue streams [1]
Nasdaq compliance regained above $1.00/share by June 8, 2026 — preserving exchange listing would remove a near-term institutional investor access risk and stabilize the equity capital structure
key_risks: Facility restart failure at New Rise Renewables Reno — the plant already required a post-commissioning shutdown; any further technical failures in HEFA processing or feedstock procurement would exhaust remaining liquidity and force additional dilutive financing or insolvency proceedings [1]

Nasdaq delisting on June 8, 2026 if the stock fails to close above $1.00 — delisting would eliminate institutional ownership eligibility, reduce liquidity, and trigger forced selling by index funds, compounding equity value impairment [2]

Lender or landlord enforcement action — the forbearance agreement with the ground lease landlord expires January 2027, and any escalation from the lender dispute before the business combination closes could foreclose on New Rise Reno and effectively eliminate equity value [2][3]
